paper.title: The Disappearance of Rituals: A Topology of the Present
paper.abstract_preview: The first time I bought something at the Japanese clothing retailer Uniqlo, I casually handed the cashier my credit card, barely grasped in one hand, while I riffled through my bag with the other. He ...

paper_type.summary: A critique of Byung-Chul Han's conception of ritual that challenges his nostalgic view and argues that longing for lost rituals obscures how ritual aesthetics function within contemporary capitalism.

concepts.name: liturgy of emptiness
concepts.definition: Han's concept describing how intense formalism in ritual practices displaces longing for meaning through overabundance of signifiers

concepts.name: deritualized present
concepts.definition: The contemporary condition characterized by the absence or loss of meaningful ritual practices

concepts.name: ritual nostalgia as colonial lever
concepts.definition: The argument that nostalgia for ritual and attempts to capture its affects served as mechanisms for early modern European colonialism and global economic expansion

relationships.explanation: Reklis directly challenges Han's conception of ritual by arguing that his Uniqlo experience contradicts Han's claim that Japanese ritual formalism dispels capitalist commodity logic
Reklis critiques Han's reliance on Barthes's experience of Japanese parcels rather than contemporary direct experience

claims.claim: Han's conception of ritual is based on nostalgia and historical distance rather than contemporary observation
Corporate entities like Uniqlo deliberately exploit ritual aesthetics and formalism for commercial purposes
Nostalgia for ritual served as a mechanism for early modern European colonialism and global economic expansion
The assumption that ritual is lost obscures how longing for ritual functions in contemporary capitalism

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to critical examination of contemporary ritual theory and its relationship to capitalist aesthetics

paper.title: Decolonial research methodology: an assessment of the challenge to established practice
paper.abstract_preview: The decolonization project challenges the hegemony of Western knowledge and colonial ways of thinking. This article explores this challenge specifically in relation to research methodology. It notes h...

paper_type.summary: This paper analyzes how decolonization challenges Western research methodology and proposes alternative approaches for indigenous and minority group research.

concepts.name: decolonial research methodology
concepts.definition: Research approaches that challenge Western knowledge hegemony and better protect the interests of indigenous and 'Other' minority groups

concepts.name: positivistic/modernist/universalist approaches
concepts.definition: Western research approaches criticized for inappropriate use when studying indigenous and minority communities

concepts.name: transformational paradigm
concepts.definition: Research paradigm that typically involves qualitative methods and participatory action research as alternatives to Western approaches

relationships.explanation: The abstract explicitly states that decolonization 'challenges the hegemony of Western knowledge' and exposes 'inappropriate use' of Western approaches
The abstract states that alternative approaches 'are generally based on constructivist principles'

claims.claim: Decolonization challenges the hegemony of Western knowledge and colonial ways of thinking in research methodology
Alternative decolonial approaches are generally based on constructivist principles and involve qualitative methods
Western positivistic approaches are inappropriately used by researchers studying indigenous and minority groups

paper.title: When decolonization is hijacked
paper.abstract_preview: Abstract This article asks how we should reconceptualize decolonization when it is hijacked by authoritarian/fascist forces. It focuses on the notorious Bhima Koregaon case in India in which 16 intell...

paper_type.summary: This paper reconceptualizes decolonization theory by examining how authoritarian regimes hijack decolonial language, using the Bhima Koregaon case in India as an empirical example.

concepts.name: hijacked decolonization
concepts.definition: The appropriation of decolonization discourse by authoritarian or fascist forces for their own political ends

concepts.name: resymbolization of colonial artifacts
concepts.definition: The process by which colonized peoples reinterpret colonial symbols and artifacts to resist oppression by native elites

concepts.name: state and global capital nexus
concepts.definition: The interconnected processes of domination involving state power and global capitalism that create cultural, psychological, political, and economic oppression

concepts.name: internal differentiation within marginalized communities
concepts.definition: The creation of hierarchies and divisions within oppressed groups that complicate unified concepts of indigenous identity

relationships.explanation: The paper explicitly calls for emancipatory politics such as 'decolonizing anthropology' to center global processes and local nuances
The paper explicitly proposes that 'contemporary decolonization debates center processes of domination and oppression'

claims.claim: Authoritarian regimes can hijack decolonization discourse while colonized peoples simultaneously resymbolize colonial artifacts to oppose native elite oppression
The most important anticolonial intellectuals may not use decolonization language and are found in social movements and prisons rather than universities
Contemporary decolonization should focus on domination processes created by the state and global capital nexus that entrench casteist/racist hierarchies
Decolonization processes work through Indigenous elites and create internal differentiation within marginalized communities

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to theoretical refinement of decolonization discourse by highlighting its potential appropriation by authoritarian forces

paper.title: Decolonising Research for Justice: Ethical Imperatives and Practical Applications
paper.abstract_preview: This article examines coloniality of research and discusses the theoretical foundations, ethical imperatives, and practical ways for conducting decolonial research for justice. It emphasises the need ...

paper_type.summary: This paper provides theoretical foundations and practical frameworks for conducting decolonial research that challenges Western knowledge hegemony and promotes epistemic justice.

concepts.name: coloniality of research
concepts.definition: The perpetuation of colonial power structures and Western knowledge dominance within research paradigms and approaches

concepts.name: decolonial research
concepts.definition: Research approaches that actively resist colonial structures and center non-Western epistemologies and voices

concepts.name: transformative decolonial praxis
concepts.definition: Practical application of decolonial principles that transforms research practice through defiance of hierarchical and extractive approaches

concepts.name: disobedient and defiant research
concepts.definition: Research approaches that actively resist and challenge dominant Western research paradigms and methodologies

concepts.name: epistemic justice
concepts.definition: Justice that addresses the marginalization and delegitimization of non-Western knowledge systems and epistemological traditions

relationships.to: scholars addressing disobedient and defiant research
relationships.explanation: The abstract explicitly states the article draws on the works of scholars addressing disobedient and defiant research

relationships.to: broader discourse on decolonising knowledge production
relationships.explanation: The abstract states the article contributes to the broader discourse on decolonising knowledge production

claims.claim: Research paradigms and approaches perpetuate coloniality and should be shifted away from
Western knowledge systems maintain hegemony and dominance while marginalizing other epistemological traditions
Decolonial research requires defiance and resistance against non-relational, hierarchical, and extractive practices
Researchers should integrate decolonial principles and frameworks into their research practices

historical_significance.lasting_impact: contributes to ongoing methodological discussions in decolonial research but impact unclear from abstract alone

paper.title: Transculturality and the Eco-Logic of Memory
paper.abstract_preview: Abstract This article asks how memory studies might use the conceptual insights gained during its “third transcultural phase” for the challenges of its “fourth phase” – heralded in this Special Issue ...

paper_type.summary: This paper proposes conceptual frameworks for integrating transcultural memory studies with environmental concerns through an 'eco-logical' approach to collective memory.

concepts.name: eco-logical memory
concepts.definition: A theoretical approach positioning memory as fundamentally ecological and relational, extending beyond human-centered perspectives

concepts.name: transcultural memory
concepts.definition: An approach to memory studies that examines memory across cultural boundaries and emphasizes relational dynamics

concepts.name: the relationality of the remembered
concepts.definition: One of three conceptual building blocks for eco-logical mnemohistories, focusing on relational aspects of remembered content

concepts.name: relational remembering
concepts.definition: A conceptual building block for eco-logical mnemohistories emphasizing the relational processes of memory formation

concepts.name: mnemonic relationality
concepts.definition: A conceptual building block for eco-logical mnemohistories focusing on the relational nature of memory itself

concepts.name: more-than-human collective memory
concepts.definition: An approach to collective memory that extends beyond human-centered perspectives to include environmental and non-human elements

relationships.from: Transculturality and the Eco-Logic of Memory
relationships.explanation: The paper explicitly states it uses insights from transcultural memory research to address new environmental challenges

relationships.from: Transculturality and the Eco-Logic of Memory
relationships.explanation: The abstract mentions that transcultural studies has roots in research on colonialism and postcolonialism

relationships.from: Transculturality and the Eco-Logic of Memory
relationships.explanation: The abstract identifies cultural globalization as one of the roots of transcultural studies that this work draws upon

claims.claim: Memory is fundamentally 'eco-logical'
Relational dynamics from transcultural memory research can enable understanding of the more-than-human in collective memory
Memory studies experienced a transcultural turn around 2010

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Proposes new theoretical frameworks for integrating environmental concerns into memory studies, but impact assessment requires full text analysis

paper.title: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation
paper.authors: Esteve Corbera, Sara Maestre‐Andrés, Yolanda Ariadne Collins, Mathew Bukhi Mabele, Dan Brockington
paper.abstract_preview: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation science and practice involves a transition towards more locally rooted, plural, socially just, and convivial forms of conservation, moving away from mainstream c...

paper_type.summary: This paper introduces and reviews contributions to a special issue on decolonizing biodiversity conservation, identifying six principles for moving away from Eurocentric conservation approaches.

concepts.name: decolonizing conservation
concepts.definition: A transition towards more locally rooted, plural, socially just, and convivial forms of conservation, moving away from mainstream conservation approaches rooted in Eurocentric ontologies and epistemologies

concepts.name: recognition principle
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

concepts.name: reparation principle
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

concepts.name: epistemic disobedience
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

concepts.name: relationality principle
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

concepts.name: power subversion principle
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

concepts.name: limits principle
concepts.definition: One of six principles identified as starting points for decolonizing conservation efforts

relationships.explanation: The abstract explicitly states this approach moves away from mainstream conservation approaches like protected areas and market-based instruments
The abstract states that decolonizing conservation moves away from approaches strongly rooted in Eurocentric ontologies and epistemologies

claims.claim: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation involves transitioning towards more locally rooted, plural, socially just, and convivial forms of conservation
Six principles (recognition, reparation, epistemic disobedience, relationality, power subversion, and limits) can serve as starting points for decolonizing conservation
Implementation of these principles can result in subtler and less universalizing conservation approaches

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Provides framework for critiquing and reforming conservation practice, though long-term impact unclear from abstract alone

paper.title: Ethical futures in biological anthropology: Research, teaching, community engagement, and curation involving deceased individuals
paper.authors: Carlina de la Cova, Courtney A. Hofman, Kathryn E. Marklein, Sabrina B. Sholts, Rachel Watkins, et al. (+2)
paper.abstract_preview: Although ethical reforms in biological anthropology have gained ground in recent years, there is still a scarcity of ethical standards for work involving historical documented collections (HDCs) at US...

paper_type.summary: This paper summarizes workshop discussions on ethical guidelines for research, teaching, and curation involving historical documented collections of deceased individuals in biological anthropology.

concepts.name: historical documented collections (HDCs)
concepts.definition: Collections of deceased individuals created in the late 19th to mid-20th centuries under anatomy laws that targeted socially marginalized communities and allowed for dissection without consent

concepts.name: ethical standards for HDCs
concepts.definition: Proposed ethical guidelines for research, teaching, community engagement, and curation involving historical documented collections

concepts.name: anatomy laws targeting marginalized communities
concepts.definition: Late 19th to mid-20th century legal frameworks that allowed dissection of socially marginalized individuals without their consent

relationships.to: recent ethical reforms in biological anthropology
relationships.explanation: The abstract states this work recognizes and builds upon recent ethical reforms that have gained ground in the field

claims.claim: There is still a scarcity of ethical standards for work involving historical documented collections at US museums and universities
Historical documented collections were created under anatomy laws that targeted socially marginalized communities without their consent
These collections have served as foundational resources for theory and methods development in biological anthropology into the 21st century

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Potential to establish new ethical standards for handling historical collections, but impact assessment requires full text and field reception analysis

paper.title: Endogenous Colonial Borders: Precolonial States and Geography in the Partition of Africa
paper.authors: Jack Paine, Xiaoyan Qiu, Joan Ricart‐Huguet
paper.abstract_preview: We revise the conventional wisdom that Africa’s international borders were drawn arbitrarily. Europeans knew very little about most of Africa in the mid-1880s, but their self-interested goals of amass...

paper_type.summary: This paper challenges the conventional wisdom that African colonial borders were arbitrary by demonstrating that Europeans actively incorporated precolonial political structures and geographical features into border formation.

concepts.name: endogenous colonial borders
concepts.definition: Colonial borders that were influenced by local African political structures and geographical conditions rather than being arbitrarily imposed

concepts.name: causal process observations for bilateral borders
concepts.definition: Systematic coding of historical evidence to trace the causal mechanisms behind each pair of countries' shared border formation

concepts.name: arbitrary African borders thesis
concepts.definition: The conventional wisdom that Africa's international borders were drawn without regard to local conditions or African input

relationships.explanation: The abstract explicitly states they 'revise the conventional wisdom that Africa's international borders were drawn arbitrarily'

claims.claim: Historical political frontiers directly affected 62% of all bilateral borders in Africa
Europeans negotiated with African rulers and learned about historical state frontiers, enabling African influence on border formation
Water bodies comprised the primary border feature much more frequently than straight lines
Europeans' self-interested goals of amassing territory prompted intensive examination of on-the-ground conditions during border formation

historical_significance.lasting_impact: challenges established narrative about African border formation but paradigm shift determination requires broader field reception analysis

paper.title: Doing migration studies with an accent
paper.abstract_preview: I use accent as a metaphor and a conceptual tool to epistemologically reflect over the field of migration studies. Accented thinking is a response and a reaction to the condition of coloniality that s...

paper_type.summary: The paper introduces 'accented thinking' as a conceptual tool to challenge colonial knowledge production structures in migration studies and develop alternative epistemological approaches.

concepts.name: accented thinking
concepts.definition: An epistemological approach that uses accent as both a way of knowing and a form of struggle against colonial knowledge production structures

concepts.name: accent as refusal
concepts.definition: Using accent as a tool to scrutinize epistemic injustices and create alternative ways of knowing

concepts.name: epistemic refusal
concepts.definition: A rejection of dominant knowledge production methods that results from combining accent as a way of knowing with accent as a form of struggle

concepts.name: coloniality of knowledge production
concepts.definition: The colonial structures that shape how knowledge is produced in academic fields

relationships.explanation: The paper positions accented thinking as a response to coloniality in knowledge production, suggesting engagement with decolonial scholarship

claims.claim: Accented thinking can stimulate the emergence of new perspectives and approaches in migration studies
The field of migration studies is structured by colonial conditions that affect knowledge production
Accent can function as both a way of knowing and a form of struggle

paper.title: Refusal (and Repair)
paper.abstract_preview: This article focuses on the concept of refusal, particularly as it has been developed within critical Black studies and critical Indigenous studies within anthropology and beyond. It argues that while...

paper_type.summary: This paper argues for reformulating anthropology through the concept of refusal as developed in critical Black and Indigenous studies to better address ongoing coloniality and racism.

concepts.name: refusal
concepts.definition: A concept developed within critical Black studies and critical Indigenous studies that provides analytical inroads for understanding political struggle beyond traditional Foucauldian and Gramscian frameworks

concepts.name: afterlives of conquest
concepts.definition: The ongoing effects and manifestations of historical conquest that continue to shape contemporary political life

concepts.name: foundational processes of modern personhood
concepts.definition: The basic processes that established the possibilities for modern personhood and political life globally, not just in the West

relationships.explanation: The paper argues that while Foucauldian frames have generated exquisite analyses of power, they have not sufficiently attended to foundational processes of modern personhood
Similar to Foucauldian frames, Gramscian approaches are noted as having limitations in addressing foundational processes that the refusal concept can address
The paper explicitly states it focuses on refusal as developed within critical Black studies
The paper explicitly states it focuses on refusal as developed within critical Indigenous studies

claims.claim: Foucauldian and Gramscian frameworks have not sufficiently attended to foundational processes that charted possibilities of modern personhood and political life globally
These frameworks have not adequately acknowledged genealogies of Black and Indigenous radical thought informing political approaches in these communities
Any significant reformulation of anthropology must recognize and foreground ongoing coloniality and racism as afterlives of conquest
Refusal provides analytical inroads for reformulating anthropological approaches to political struggle

historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to ongoing efforts to decolonize anthropological theory and method
